title_en: Marriage and Transactions Disputes in Al-Insaf
title_ar: مسائل الخلاف في النكاح والمعاملات في الإنصاف
content_en:
Al-Mardawi's treatment of marriage disputes in Al-Insaf addresses the many areas within Hanbali family law where multiple transmitted opinions from Imam Ahmad or his students have generated ongoing scholarly debate. His resolutions of these disputes provide the definitive Hanbali positions that later scholars like Ibn Qudamah and al-Buhuti built upon.

The question of whether a woman's silence constitutes consent to marriage when approached through her guardian is treated with nuance. The famous hadith — 'The virgin is asked for her permission, and her silence is her consent' (al-Bukhari, Muslim) — is the basis, but the Hanbali school has differences on the exact circumstances in which silence counts as consent and when explicit verbal acceptance is required. Al-Mardawi identifies the conditions under which silence suffices and when explicit consent is needed.

The validity of a marriage contracted without the bride's knowledge but with her guardian's consent is a question generating multiple Hanbali views. Imam Ahmad's transmitted positions differ, reflecting the tension between the contract-nature of marriage (which may be contracted by a guardian on behalf of his ward in some legal contexts) and the personal nature of the spousal relationship requiring genuine consent. Al-Mardawi's resolution carefully balances these considerations.

On the minimum mahr, the Hanbali school disputes exist between those who set no minimum (any agreed value) and those who set a minimum analogous to other schools' positions. Al-Mardawi affirms that no minimum is set in the Hanbali school — any amount of measurable value constitutes a valid mahr — based on the hadith of the man who could offer nothing but teaching the bride Quran (which the Prophet accepted as a mahr).

In commercial transactions, the Hanbali school's questions about the validity of time-limited contracts generate internal dispute. The Hanbali school generally requires contracts to be permanent or for a specified term — open-ended conditions create gharar. Al-Mardawi examines multiple forms of conditional sale and assesses which conditions validly modify the contract and which ones render it invalid due to excessive uncertainty.

The question of profit-sharing in muzara'ah (sharecropping) arrangements generates extensive Hanbali discussion. Imam Ahmad permitted sharecropping in which the landowner provides the land and the farmer provides labor, sharing the output in an agreed proportion — based on the prophetic precedent of the arrangement with the Jews of Khaybar. Al-Mardawi resolves the internal disputes about which terms make such a contract valid and which ones introduce prohibited gharar.

Al-Mardawi closes with a reflection on the purpose of Al-Insaf: to serve the student and scholar of Hanbali fiqh by providing a reliable compass through the school's internal scholarly diversity. He quotes Imam Ahmad's reported statement: 'Do not follow me blindly, nor Malik, nor ash-Shafi'i, nor al-Awza'i, nor ath-Thawri. Learn from where they learned.' This captures the spirit of Hanbali scholarship at its best — fidelity to the Quran and Sunnah as the ultimate source, with the imam's guidance as a trusted map rather than an infallible oracle.
